routes.description: Read the full bridge status when you need a complete operational snapshot. This includes notebook identity, server config, execution state, debug state, and other bridge runtime details. Use it for higher-risk reasoning or troubleshooting, not as the default preflight for every notebook action.

Use this as the default notebook preflight. It returns the active notebook identity, selection, version token, current server, and busy or idle signal without dragging full context into the model. Call this before most normal notebook mutations or targeted runs, and only escalate to heavier status or context tools when notebook identity or routing is unclear.
Alias of bridge_get_status_brief. Use it only if your client expects this shorter tool name; otherwise prefer bridge_get_status_brief directly.

List local bridge servers discovered across the configured port span. Use this when multiple VS Code windows or notebooks may be open and you need to inspect candidates before pinning a specific server. Do not use it as a default preflight when the current automatic server match is already stable.

Diagnostic-only command listing. Use this when debugging bridge internals or checking command exposure, not as a normal notebook-work preflight. Routine MCP use should rely on the registered tool surface instead of probing commands first.

Diagnostic-only capability probe. Use this when the task is explicitly about bridge implementation, route support, or transport debugging. Do not call this during routine notebook work when the MCP tool surface is already available.

Read bridge compliance state when you need to know whether the current workflow is expected to remain bridge-backed and whether fallback is allowed. This is useful for policy checks, troubleshooting, and higher-risk notebook operations. It is not necessary as a default preflight for every routine mutation or run.

Read notebook-level metadata and, when explicitly requested, notebook source or outputs. Use this when you need notebook-wide information rather than cell-level planning. Prefer lighter cell or status tools for routine work so you do not pull unnecessary content into context.

Check whether the current notebook has unsaved changes. Use this before save or revert decisions, or when you need to know whether bridge mutations have made the notebook dirty. It is not needed as a standard preflight for ordinary reads or runs.

List notebook cells in a lightweight form. By default it returns only the fields that help an agent plan the next step, such as index, kind, language, source summary, output summary, and execution summary. Request full source, metadata, or outputs only when the next step actually depends on them.

Read one target cell by index, id, marker, or current selection. Use this before editing an existing cell so you can inspect the current content and carry the returned readToken into a follow-up mutation; the bridge will reject stale updates if that token no longer matches. Heavy fields are opt-in so this can stay cheap during normal notebook work.

Return a combined snapshot of notebook identity, kernel state, mutation state, execution state, compliance state, and lightweight cell summaries. Use this for higher-risk reasoning, debugging, or notebook drift investigation. Do not use it as the default preflight for every low-risk notebook step.

Read kernel connection and runtime state for the active notebook. Use this when kernel availability, connectivity, or lifecycle details matter to the next step. Prefer status brief or execution state for ordinary notebook work when you do not need kernel-specific details.
Alias of bridge_get_kernel_state. Use it only if your caller expects the shorter path-backed variant.

Read the full output payload for a target cell. Use this only when the next reasoning step truly needs raw output content rather than a short summary, since full outputs can be large and expensive in context. Prefer bridge_get_output_summary for normal confirmation and lightweight inspection.

Read the smallest useful view of a cell's outputs. This is the default way to confirm whether a recent execution produced outputs, errors, or a short textual summary without pulling the full output payload into context. It also reports whether the target is still pending, completed with no output, or completed with output. Prefer this over the full output endpoint unless you truly need raw outputs.

Read execution observation state for the active notebook or for a specific operationId. Use this after a run or restart request when you need to know whether work is still pending, whether completion has been observed, or whether output observation is pending. It supports waitFor with the standard values `completion`, `output`, or `stable`; `idle` is accepted as a legacy alias of `stable`. Pair it with timeoutMs to await progress without hand-written sleep loops.

Read current debug-session state and metadata. Use this when a notebook debugging session is active or when you need to confirm debug status before issuing continue, step, or stop actions. It is not part of the normal non-debug notebook path.

Advanced passthrough for allowed VS Code commands. Use this only when a higher-level notebook tool does not already express the action you need, or when you are explicitly debugging command-level behavior. This should not be the default path for normal notebook mutation or execution work.

Execute one cell by explicit index. Use this when the target index is already known and you want a direct, compact execution request without extra selection logic. Prefer the regular run tools when their names match your intent more clearly.

Resolve and read a cell through a POST body rather than query parameters. Use this when the locator is easier to express as structured JSON, such as with nested fields or generated request payloads. It behaves like bridge_get_cell and is especially useful in scripted or batched workflows.

Apply several related cell operations in one request. Use this when building or reorganizing one notebook stage and you want fewer round trips than one tool call per cell. Keep batches small, usually 2-4 closely related cells, instead of dumping an entire notebook at once. Each operation should normally include `op`, using one of: `append`, `insert`, `update`, `delete`, `move`, `duplicate`, `select`, `reveal`, `replaceOutputs`, or `clearOutputs`. For pure new-cell payloads that only provide source/kind metadata and no locator, `append` is inferred automatically. The default mode is transactional, which plans against a notebook snapshot, verifies source-bearing writes after apply, and aborts cleanly on invalid operations; use bestEffort only when partial success is genuinely acceptable. Existing-cell operations that can race with outside edits should carry readToken protection.

Insert a new cell at a specific index. Use this for precise notebook structuring when you already know where the new cell belongs. The default response is compact; read the cell afterward only if the next step needs the newly created cell's full content or readToken.

Append a new cell to the end of the notebook. Use this when building a notebook stage sequentially and the new cell should simply go last. Prefer this over manual index arithmetic when the exact end position is what you want.

Update one existing cell's source, kind, or metadata. Before calling this tool on an existing cell, read that cell first and pass the returned readToken back here so the bridge can reject stale edits instead of overwriting newer notebook content. The default response is intentionally compact and should be paired with separate state or output reads when you need more detail.

Delete one or more cells. Use this when the target cells are already known and the notebook structure should shrink. For deleting a single existing cell that may have changed, prefer carrying a recent readToken so the delete is based on a fresh read.

Move a target cell to a new index. Use this when reorganizing notebook structure without rewriting cell content. If the source cell was previously read and may have changed since then, pass the readToken so the move is guarded against stale targeting.

Duplicate a target cell. Use this when you want to preserve an existing working cell while creating a nearby variant for experimentation or explanation. If the source cell was previously read and may have changed, pass the readToken to keep duplication aligned with the expected source.

Select a target cell in the active notebook editor. Use this when the next user-visible or command-visible action depends on selection state. Do not overuse it when explicit index-based tools already express the action without needing selection changes.

Reveal a target cell in the notebook editor without changing selection intent. Use this for UI guidance or follow behavior when you want the editor to scroll to the target cell. It is a UI convenience tool, not a required step before ordinary mutation or execution.

Replace outputs for a target cell. Use this mainly for notebook maintenance, testing, or controlled output injection rather than normal notebook execution. If the target cell was previously read and may have changed, pass the readToken to avoid stale output replacement.

Clear outputs for a target cell or for the whole notebook when `all` is set. Use this before reruns, cleanup, or when the next step depends on seeing fresh outputs only. Prefer the narrower cell-level clear when a whole-notebook clear is unnecessary.
Alias of bridge_post_cell_clear_outputs. Use it only if a caller expects the output-scoped route form.

Use this when you need to update an existing cell and immediately run it as one bridge-backed step. Pass the last readToken to protect against stale edits. The default response is compact and returns an execution ticket; only request observation or output when the next reasoning step truly depends on it. This should replace ad-hoc multi-call sequences when the target cell is already known.

Use this when you need to insert a new cell and immediately execute it. This is especially useful for stage-by-stage notebook building, where a new setup or analysis cell should be created and run without several separate transport calls. The default response stays compact and returns an execution ticket; only request observation or full output when needed.

Execute the currently selected cell. Use this only when selection is already intentional and stable; otherwise prefer a tool that targets an explicit cell. The response is compact on purpose, so read execution state or output summary separately if you need confirmation details.

Execute one known target cell. Use this after notebook identity and cell targeting are already clear, then read execution state or output summary separately if you need confirmation details. The response is intentionally compact so normal execution requests do not drag large state payloads back into context.

Execute cells above a target cell. Use this when a target cell depends on setup or precursor cells that should be rerun without executing the entire notebook. Prefer this over run-all when only the upstream portion matters.

Execute a target cell and the cells below it. Use this when a stage should be rerun from a known checkpoint downward. Prefer this over run-all when the upper notebook structure does not need to be rerun.

Execute the entire notebook. This is a higher-risk and higher-noise action than targeted execution, so prefer stage-based or cell-based runs first. Do not use it as the first meaningful validation step for a fresh notebook build; keep it for explicit user requests or final integration passes after stage-level validation.

Execute the selected cell and move selection to the next cell. Use this when you want a notebook-like step-through workflow inside the active editor. It is more UI-coupled than explicit index-based run tools, so avoid it when selection is not already intentional.

Execute precedent cells for the target cell. Use this when dependency-aware upstream execution is more appropriate than a simple index-based range. This is helpful when notebook dependency semantics matter more than visual position alone.

Execute dependent cells for the target cell. Use this when a change to one cell should be propagated through cells that rely on it, without rerunning unrelated notebook sections. This is more selective than a full run-below when dependency structure is important.

Start notebook debugging for a target cell. Use this only when the task is explicitly about debugging behavior or interactive investigation. Normal notebook execution flows should stay on the regular run tools.
Continue the active debug session. Use this only after a debug session has already started and debug state indicates there is something to continue.
Step over in the active debug session. Use this for interactive debugging when execution is paused and you need one controlled forward step. It is not part of ordinary notebook execution.
Stop the active debug session. Use this when the debug session is no longer needed or should be terminated cleanly. It should normally be paired with debug-state awareness, not used blindly.

Interrupt the current notebook kernel. Use this when the notebook is stuck, overlong, or needs to be stopped immediately. Because it affects the shared runtime, do not use it as a routine control step.

Restart the current notebook kernel. Use this when state needs to be reset or the kernel is unhealthy. Because restart discards runtime state, prefer narrower actions when a full reset is unnecessary. The response returns an operationId plus submitted/acknowledged semantics; query execution state when you need to know whether the restart is still pending or has been acknowledged.

Restart the kernel and run the entire notebook. Use this only for explicit user requests or full integration passes where a clean, from-scratch rerun is needed. It is heavier and riskier than targeted execution. The response returns an operationId plus submitted/acknowledged semantics so callers can inspect follow-up state explicitly.

Restart the kernel and run up to a target cell. Use this when you need a clean runtime state but only need execution through a specific checkpoint rather than the entire notebook. This is useful for deterministic staged rebuilds. The response returns an operationId plus submitted/acknowledged semantics so callers can inspect follow-up state explicitly.

Declared kernel-shutdown route that is intentionally unsupported right now. Use this only when a caller needs to detect or surface that limitation explicitly. Do not assume it will perform a real shutdown; it exists so clients can reason about the unsupported state in a stable way.
Open the kernel selector UI. Use this when the task explicitly needs kernel choice or when the current kernel is missing or wrong. It is an interactive editor action, not a standard preflight step.

Save the current notebook editor. Use this when you want to persist bridge-applied notebook changes to disk, especially before handing the notebook off or ending a multi-step task. It is not necessary after every tiny bridge action unless the task explicitly requires eager saves.

Revert the current notebook editor to the last saved state. Use this with care, only when the task truly requires discarding unsaved notebook changes. It is a destructive reset of unsaved work and should not be used casually.

Close the current notebook editor tab. Use this when the workflow explicitly calls for leaving the notebook editor, not as a normal end-of-task action. Closing the editor is independent from kernel shutdown.

Bring the current notebook editor into focus. Use this when UI state matters, such as aligning the visible editor with upcoming notebook actions or user observation. It is not required for ordinary bridge-backed mutations or reads.

Open the variable viewer in the active notebook context. Use this when the task explicitly needs interactive inspection of notebook variables or runtime state. It is a UI support action, not part of the minimal read-mutate-run loop.

Open the Jupyter Data Viewer. Use this when the task explicitly calls for dataframe-style exploration or interactive table inspection. It is not required for ordinary bridge-backed notebook reasoning.

Open the Jupyter output panel. Use this when user-visible output inspection in the VS Code UI is important, especially for debugging or large-output workflows. It is a UI helper rather than a core notebook reasoning tool.
Open the interpreter selector. Use this when the active notebook environment or interpreter must be changed or verified interactively. It is not part of the normal compact notebook execution path.

mcpTools.description: List bridge servers when multiple VS Code windows or notebook sessions may be open. Use this to inspect candidate servers, active notebooks, and workspaces before pinning a specific target. It is a routing and disambiguation tool, not something that should be called before every normal notebook step.

Return the bridge server currently selected for this MCP session. Use this when you need to confirm whether the session is in automatic mode or pinned to a specific override. It is mainly useful for routing clarity and debugging, not as a required preflight before routine notebook actions.

Pin the current MCP session to a specific bridge server using either a baseUrl or a serverId returned by bridge_list_servers. Use this when automatic matching is ambiguous or incorrect. Keep the override only as long as needed, then clear it to return to automatic best-match selection.

Clear the current MCP server override and return the session to automatic bridge selection. Use this after a temporary pin once the ambiguity is gone. There is no need to call it if the session was never overridden.
